title: Paper Clay Nature Cast Wall Tiles
subtitle: A set of three high-relief nature impression tiles
excerpt: Press leaves, bark, and seed heads deeply into paper clay slabs to create a set of three high-relief wall tiles. No kiln required. PVA sealed and wall-mounted with adhesive pads once dry.
sourceNotes: Technique derived from paper clay slab-building and nature impression methods.

glossaryTerms.definition: Air-dry clay with paper fibres mixed in, which adds tensile strength and reduces cracking, making it well suited to thin slabs and tiles
A surface decoration where the impressed or modelled elements project clearly from the background, creating strong shadows and visible depth
The stage at which clay holds its shape and no longer sticks to your hands, but still contains moisture and can be carved or smoothed
A flat sheet of clay rolled to an even thickness, used here as the tile base

content.text: Paper clay
is the ideal material for these tiles because its paper-fibre content reduces cracking in thin
slab
work. Each tile in this set of three is pressed with a different natural object (a leaf, a piece of bark, a dried seed head) to create striking
high-relief
impressions. Once dried and sealed with PVA, they are hung as a trio using adhesive pads. No kiln needed.

materials.name: Bark pieces, dried seed heads, or other firm botanicals
Adhesive picture pads or picture-hanging strips

content.text: Preparation

Collect your natural objects. Fresh leaves with pronounced veins work best for fine detail. Flat or gently curved bark sections and dried seed heads give bolder texture. Allow bark to flatten slightly by placing under a heavy book for a few hours if it is curved.
Decide on a consistent tile size for all three. Tiles 15 x 10 cm work well together. Cut three card rectangles at this size as size guides.
Knead the paper clay for 2 minutes until smooth. Paper clay is slightly stiffer than standard air-dry clay; work it thoroughly to avoid surface cracking.
Rolling the tiles
Place clay on baking paper. Roll with the pottery rolling pin using 8 mm guide sticks to produce a
slab
8 mm thick. Tiles this thick are sturdy enough to hang without cracking.
Lay a card size guide on the slab and cut around it with the fettling knife to produce your first tile rectangle. Repeat for all three tiles.
Smooth the cut edges by running a damp fingertip along them.
Pressing in the naturals
Lay your botanical object on the first tile, arranging it to fill the space. For a
high-relief
impression, place a sheet of baking paper over the object and use the rolling pin to press it firmly into the clay. For a 3-4 mm deep impression, apply real downward pressure.
Remove the baking paper. Peel the botanical away carefully. The deeper the impression, the more it may resist removal; work slowly from one end.
Repeat for each of the three tiles with a different natural object on each.
Drying and sealing

Leave the tiles flat on baking paper. Cover with a plastic bag for the first 12 hours to prevent warping. Paper clay is more warp-resistant than standard air-dry clay, but slow drying still helps. Remove the bag once at
leather-hard
stage and leave to dry fully. Allow 3 days for 8 mm tiles.
Flip each tile once it is fully dry to seal the back face with PVA too. This helps keep moisture from entering from behind when hung.
Apply two coats of diluted PVA (1:1 with water) to all surfaces, working the brush into the deep impression recesses. Allow 1 hour between coats.
Once dry, mount each tile on the wall using adhesive picture pads rated for the weight of the tile. Clay at 8 mm thickness and 15 x 10 cm will weigh approximately 150-200 g per tile.
Variations
Wash the sealed tile with diluted acrylic paint and wipe back from the high points with a damp cloth to highlight the relief.
Make a set of five or more tiles as a feature wall panel.
Trim tiles into circles or irregular shapes before drying for a less formal grouping.
Press bark texture over the whole slab before cutting tiles for an all-over tactile background.

items.problem: Tile warped into a curve during drying
items.cause: One face dried faster than the other
items.fix: Flip the tile at leather-hard stage so both faces dry evenly. Stack tiles under a board with gentle weight for the first day.

items.problem: Botanical refused to release cleanly from the clay
items.cause: Object was pressed too deeply or clay was too soft
items.fix: Work the object free from one edge using the needle tool. Next time, press into slightly firmer clay or use lighter pressure.

items.problem: Tile cracked along one edge when drying
items.cause: Edge dried faster than the centre
items.fix: Slow the drying with a plastic bag cover and ensure the baking paper is flat under the tile. Repair hairline cracks with a slurry of paper clay before sealing.

items.problem: Adhesive pads will not support the tile weight
items.cause: Standard pads were used rather than heavy-duty picture pads
items.fix: Use picture pads rated to at least 500 g and apply two pads per tile. Allow the pads to cure for 24 hours before hanging.
